title: The Academy — Program Overview
content: The Academy is an after-school and weekend enrichment program serving K-12 students in under-resourced communities. The Academy pairs trained near-peer tutors with students for strengths-based, project-driven learning. Rather than drilling deficits, coaches identify each student's top skills — whether that's storytelling, design, coding, or math — and build lesson plans that lead with those strengths. The program runs cohorts throughout the year (Fall, Spring, and Summer). Each cohort includes weekly sessions, monthly skill showcases, and an end-of-cohort capstone presentation open to families.

title: Enrollment and Onboarding
content: Families can enroll students online via the program website or in person at any Academy site. Enrollment opens a few weeks before each cohort begins. Required documents include a completed application form, a copy of the student's most recent report card, and a signed family agreement. There is no cost to families — The Academy is fully grant-funded. After enrollment, each student completes a skills assessment administered by a lead coach. Assessment results are entered into the student skills database (accessible via the n8n webhook integration) and used to assign the student to a coaching team and to generate their first personalized lesson plan.

title: The Academy Coaching Model
content: Academy coaches are trained in strengths-based facilitation. Every session begins with a check-in (what's one thing going well?) and ends with a reflection (what did you build or learn today?). Coaches use the student's recorded strengths — drawn from the skills database — to design activities that feel immediately relevant and achievable. When a student struggles, coaches are trained to reframe the challenge as a skills puzzle: which of the student's existing strengths can unlock this new concept? Coaches are not expected to tutor every subject; they are facilitators who help students apply their own capabilities to new problems.

title: Student Skills Database and Check-In System
content: Student skill records are stored in a Google Sheet that syncs to an n8n workflow via a webhook endpoint. Each row represents one check-in event for one student and includes: row number, first name, last name, check-in date, and skill demonstrated. The AI planning assistant queries this webhook in real time to build a strength index — grouping all check-ins per student, ranking skills by frequency, and surfacing the top three strengths. This data powers the personalized lesson plan generator and the calendar milestone export feature. Coaches update the sheet directly after each session; no additional tools are required.

title: AI-Powered Planning Assistant
content: The Academy Calendar Workspace uses a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) architecture. When a coach or administrator asks a question, the assistant first searches a curated knowledge base to retrieve the most relevant program documents, policies, or student records. It then passes those retrieved chunks — along with the original question — to a language model (currently Google Gemini via OpenRouter) to synthesize a grounded, accurate answer. This approach means the assistant only answers from verified program documentation, reducing the risk of hallucinated responses. Sources are always cited so coaches can verify any answer against the original document.

title: Calendar Invites and .ics Export
content: Coaches and program managers can export lesson milestones as .ics calendar files directly from the Calendar Workspace. Each milestone becomes a separate calendar invite with the student's name, session title, and objective in the event description. Exports can be scoped to: all program events, a selected student's milestones only, or a group of students. The .ics format is compatible with Google Calendar, Apple Calendar, Outlook, and any calendar app that supports the iCalendar standard. Microsoft Calendar can also be connected directly via OAuth to push events automatically without downloading a file.

title: Coach Responsibilities and Expectations
content: Academy coaches are expected to attend all scheduled sessions for their assigned students, update the skill check-in sheet within 24 hours of each session, and complete the monthly reflections survey. Coaches who need to miss a session must notify the site coordinator in advance and arrange a substitute. Each coach is assigned a small group of students per cohort. Lead coaches oversee a team of peer coaches and are responsible for reviewing lesson plans before the start of each week. All coaches must complete the Academy Training Certificate before their first session.

title: Capstone Project Requirements
content: Every cohort concludes with a student capstone project. Students choose their own project topic in week 6, guided by their coach. Projects must demonstrate at least one of the student's recorded top strengths and must be presentable to a non-technical audience (family members, community partners). Past capstone formats have included short films, product prototypes, original research posters, coding projects, community service campaigns, and business pitches. Capstone presentations are held on the final Saturday of the cohort from 2pm to 5pm at the site. Families receive invitations four weeks before the event.

title: Student Data Privacy and FERPA Compliance
content: The Academy handles student records in compliance with FERPA and applicable state student privacy laws. Student names, skill records, and attendance data are stored in systems accessible only to credentialed Academy staff and coaches assigned to that student. No student data is shared with third parties without written family consent. The AI planning assistant accesses the skills database only to generate lesson plans and calendar exports — it does not store student data beyond the session. Family portal access allows parents and guardians to view their child's skill progress, session notes, and upcoming calendar events.

title: Program Sites and Locations
content: The Academy currently operates at multiple sites including community centers, public libraries, school partnerships, and a fully virtual site for students who cannot attend in person. Each site serves a cohort of students each term. The virtual site uses the same curriculum and check-in system but sessions are conducted over video call. Site coordinators are full-time Academy staff responsible for coach scheduling, student communication, and facility logistics.

title: Program Outcomes and Impact
content: In the most recent program year, The Academy served students across multiple sites and cohorts. Post-cohort surveys showed strong results: the majority of students reported feeling more confident in at least one academic skill, most families said their child's engagement with school improved, and nearly all coaches said the strengths-based model made their sessions more effective than traditional tutoring. Of students who completed a capstone project, most presented to a live audience of family and community members. The Academy continues to expand its reach through grant funding and community partnerships.

title: 3rd Grade Reading Skills and Benchmarks
content: By the end of 3rd grade, students are expected to master the following reading skills. Phonics and word recognition: decode multisyllabic words using prefixes, suffixes, and root words; use context clues for unfamiliar vocabulary; recognize and read irregularly spelled grade-level words. Reading fluency: read grade-level prose and poetry with accuracy, appropriate rate, and expression; target oral reading rate is 90–110 words per minute with high accuracy; use self-correction strategies when comprehension breaks down. Literary text comprehension: ask and answer questions to demonstrate understanding; recount stories including fables and folk tales; describe characters and explain how their actions drive the plot; determine the central message, lesson, or moral of a story; compare and contrast themes and settings across texts. Informational text comprehension: determine the main idea and explain how key details support it; describe relationships between historical events or scientific concepts; use domain-specific vocabulary; compare the most important points across two texts on the same topic. Writing: produce opinion, informative, and narrative writing with clear structure, supporting details, and a concluding statement. Language: use correct grammar including subject-verb agreement, plural nouns, and simple verb tenses; use context clues and dictionaries to determine word meanings.

title: Maria Santos — Student Skill Profile
content: Student: Maria Santos. Grade level: 3rd grade. Current skill level and recorded strengths: reading comprehension (top strength — demonstrated across all three most recent check-ins), phonics and decoding (second strength — coach noted strong vowel pattern recognition), and storytelling (third strength — used oral retelling activities effectively). Recent check-in notes: Maria is reading at grade level with strong literal comprehension but is developing her ability to infer meaning and identify the central message in longer texts. She excels at retelling story events in sequence. Coach recommendation: build lesson plans around Maria's storytelling strength to unlock inferencing skills — have her narrate what a character might be thinking or feeling as a bridge to deeper comprehension. Next session focus: comparing two short texts to identify the author's message, using Maria's storytelling skills as the entry point.
